So I say to the guy, you don't know me, if you knew me, you probably would be saying, let me think about it. And 90 plus out of 100 times, he'll say, yeah, that's the truth. So now what you've done is someone said, let me think about it, and you've said, let me ask you a question, does it make sense? You've rebuilt certainty with the first 10, right? So check, you then started, you actually went back to yourself. Check, you've resold yourself to the guy, you've resold your company to the guy, 10, 10, 10, and then you asked for the order. Now you're in complete control of the sale. Everyone get that concept here? That's called a loop, L-O-O-P. And the reason it's called a loop is because on some level, what you did was this. You actually went backwards and resold him, you already mentioned things about the company, you've already sold him the company once. You actually went back and resold him the company. You've resold yourself, you've resold the company, and then you asked for the order again. Everyone follow that? You don't just say to someone, let me tell you what would be idiotic. Someone says I need to think about it, and you say, smart businessmen don't really think about things for that long. They make decisions and all the information is fresh in your mind. John, give me a shot in 10,000 shares, believe me, you'll be very, very impressed. Have you done anything, have you created certainty in the three tens? No, he's not buying because of the three tens, you're answering the silly objection. You're accomplishing nothing. So what the loop is, a loop is the stuff in the middle. It's essentially these techniques and the language that we use to create certainty, which gives us the right to ask for the order again. You have no right, if someone says to you, I want to think about it, and you say, smart businessmen don't think about things, give me a shot, you haven't earned the right to ask for the order again. You're a nag, you're annoying him. If someone says, I need to think about it, oh wait, let me give you some fresh information about the company, about myself and my firm. Like, ah, now you have now, give me, I'm sorry, you didn't know that. You give them fresh info and you say, give me a shot, you won't be sorry. Everyone see the difference? You've given them a reason to change their mind, to change their mind. New information, that process of how you actually go backwards and resell the company, resell yourself, resell the idea, that's called a loop. The first loop is always the same, it's always the same. Matter what they say to you, you just say, I hear what you're saying, but does the idea make sense? Do you like the idea? That's how it starts and it ends. That will be the next place he goes, he'll switch your objectives, or, sounds good, but I'm not that liquid right now. The way you lower action threshold is this, typically it's this, some sort of guarantee. That's why when you see all these infomercials, what are they always doing? You use the money back, you say at the end, they don't say at the beginning, you don't lower action threshold in the beginning, you lower it at the end. When someone's close but not quite there, you temporarily lower their action threshold by saying, and guess what, if I'm wrong, you can give it back. It's not everything I said, you can return it, and get a refund, that's how you lower action threshold. In the stock market, you can't do that, typically. I guess in some investments you can, for the most part, you can't. So the way you lower action threshold is as follows. Okay, and I mentioned this before, but I want to touch on it a bit more intensely now, is you make your clients run a different movie. Let me explain to you what that means. Remember I said to you that, I was walking through the airport, I saw the guy take a swing of the golf club, and me, the low action threshold, I run the movie, that says, oh my god, I can imagine myself on the 17th, green, or Augusta, they're just playing amazing around the golf field, and all the wonderful things, I'm a positive thinking guy. I think of all the great stuff, that's the movie. I actually have pictures, I run a movie. I see myself teeing up on the 18th, wham, oh, feels good. I don't run a movie that says, oh my god, if I'm wrong, I'll feel stupid. I remember when I was, I once bought a thing, my wife said I'm an idiot about that, and I felt short of money, and they wouldn't return it when I called for the return. Again, I don't do that stuff. I'm like, ah, if it's bad, I don't run the negative movie. My father, on the other hand, will run a movie, like Disaster, and if I'm wrong, oh my god, I'll feel like an idiot, I'll be taken, then I'll have to be on the phone, try to return the product, I'll probably scream for that, then my wife will be mad at me, and I'll be in a fold of terrible, terrible, exaggerated downside, and then when he runs the upside, he says, yeah, I'll probably take it to the thing, and it'll suck. Probably won't even worry, he runs a very negative, upside film, everyone get that? That's some of the high action thresholds. They run a negative, a long-winded negative movie, and when they project out into the future, they run a very short movie without a lot of, you know, emotional movement, they don't get moved emotionally. Does that make sense, yes? So what you do to lower action threshold is you make them run a different movie, and the way you typically do it is say, let me ask you a question, what's the worst that can possibly happen here? Let's say I'm wrong, and the stop drops 10%, and you lose $4,000, is that gonna put you into poor house? And they'll say, no, probably, say exactly, and let's say I'm right, it goes up 20 points, and you make 20,000, it's not gonna make me the richest man, will it? They'll say, no, say exactly, but what it will do, it will serve as a benchmark for future business. It will show you that I'm a guy that's gonna put you into the market at the right time, and more importantly, take you out. It'll serve to build our track record. We're gonna base it this time. Next time I'll actually do another base hit with me, and then one more base hit, but after three base hits together, I'm gonna ask you, in fact, you're gonna demand that you step up to the plate, and let a home run together. Listen, I, that's the beginning, how you actually make them future pace a different movie about if they're wrong, the consequences are very minor, but if you're right, and you say, by the way, what I could do for you besides this one trade over the long term, the way of new issues, IPOs, warrant conversions, investment backing deals, I wanna be an asset to you and your family, and you start piling on the long term benefits, you blunt the short term movie, and then you say, why don't we do this? Instead of taking 50,000, start off small, the firm minimum is $25,000. All I'm asking for is that you can be one shot, very, very small, work with the stuff, and you actually make them run a different way. Give me some, believe me, you will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? And they're like, all right, do it, and they'll do it. Now, it works like, it's scary how well it works. Pain lowers the action threshold, which is the fifth variable of the straight line. So once we lower someone's action threshold, and we still can't get them to close, while we'll do a secondary pattern for lowering action threshold, and we'll add on pain as well. The way you do that, this process, is called looping. It's how you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close, in these distinct language patterns, and you're building certainty for the three tens, you're building certainty, and you lower action, so add on, it's a formula, a little bit of this, a little bit of that, and you're just looping back, and keep going forward, looping, maintaining control of the sale, from the open to the close, to a 10, 10, 10, I lower their action threshold, raise up their pain, bam, guess what? Everyone who is closeable will be closed. That's what the straight line is about. Now just imagine it this way. We have these five variables, right, five. Product, U, company, action, threshold, pain, five. Look at it like this, anyone see the movie, The Italian Job? It's a movie about the state crack safes. Remember when the old man, in the beginning, Donald Sutherland, he's trying to crack the safe, and he listens, and he's spinning the knob, and he has a click, right, and he spins it the other way, click, click, right? He's a safe cracker, on some level, what you're doing as a salesperson, is you're essentially cracking someone's buying combination. Okay, and every time you ask for the order, you're clicking the knob and see if the safe opens. If it doesn't open, what do you do? You spin the dial. Now what are the five numbers? In a safe, I mean 23 left, 35 right, 16, there's numbers. In buying, there's five numbers. Here are your five combinations to the safe. Before that safe's gonna click open, you have to have them to a 10, 10, 10. That's gotta be lowered to the point where it's above, this is gotta be above, these three have to be above your action threshold, and the pain threshold is like the lubricator on it. Every time you ask for the order and run up the panel, you're clicking the safe, does it open? Everyone get that? That's what you're doing. As you take someone down the straight line, essentially you're picking the lock to someone saying yes. Not everybody needs to be at a 10, 10, 10. I could be a 666. Some people need an 898, everyone's a bit different. Some people, it's like actually it's easy to get them certain, but they have a very high action threshold, it has to be lowered. We don't really know. The truth is you meet someone, you just don't know. You pick up the phone, I can't say, ah, he's a 789 with a lower, I don't know. So what do I do? I keep running patterns and going down the straight line and testing with saying give me a shot, I might ask for the order seven times, and I try to open the safe, and he says no, no, no. I keep spinning the dial, find that last one, boom, the safe opens, and I got the order. Does that make sense to everybody? So which is why when you're moving someone down the straight line from the open to the close, okay, you're not gonna ask for the order once or twice. You have to ask for the order five or six times because you don't know if you've opened, you might have opened the lock already, you haven't tested with asking for the order. So you don't ask for the order once or twice, you ask for it again and again and again, elegantly. Not like give me a shot, give me a shot, elegantly. You know, give me a shot, believe me sir, you'll be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough? Now you wouldn't keep saying you'll be very, very impressed. Sound fair, eight times because you sound like a broken record. You have different ways of asking for the amount of six or seven different language patterns for asking for the order. Everyone get that?
